docs.title: Concepts
docs.text:
Cross-cutting ideas, trends, and frameworks in market research:

Page First Observed Last Updated Description ai-democratization 2026-06-24 2026-06-25 AI democratization refers to the process where AI automates more expert tasks, leaving less expert tasks for humans. ai-professionalization 2026-06-24 2026-06-25 AI professionalization refers to the process where AI automates less expert tasks, leaving more expert tasks for humans. superstar-effect 2026-06-24 2026-06-25 The top 20% of most AI-exposed companies, referred to as 'superstars', achieve a significantly higher productivity growth rate. seniorised-roles 2026-06-25 2026-06-25 The phenomenon where entry-level roles increasingly demand skills traditionally required in senior positions due to AI adoption.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Ai democratization
docs.text: AI democratization refers to the process where AI automates more expert tasks, leaving less expert tasks for humans.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: AI democratization describes how artificial intelligence makes advanced capabilities accessible by automating complex expert tasks, thereby reducing expertise requirements for certain occupations. As quantified in Figure 2 of the PwC 2026 report, this process affects 52% of global job postings. Concrete examples include [[recruiters]] (AI automates CV screening while humans handle contract negotiation) and [[inventory-clerks]] (AI manages stock optimization while humans perform physical movement). Figure 3 demonstrates democratization is particularly strong in high AI exposure jobs, where expertise requirements decrease significantly. Crucially, Figure 11 reveals that despite reduced expertise demands, democratized roles maintain high epoch-score (0.48), indicating continued reliance on human capabilities for remaining tasks—often involving physical dexterity, adaptability, or interpersonal skills. The report warns that while democratization expands job accessibility (e.g., enabling non-experts to perform data analysis), it may compress career ladders and reduce skill development opportunities without targeted interventions. This concept represents one of two primary pathways (alongside ai-professionalization) in AI's labor market restructuring. Organizations can leverage democratization through the ai-job-future-framework's 'Supply and Demand' dimension, redesigning roles to balance AI efficiency with human execution. The trend is accelerating post-2022, as shown in Figure 9's widening skill transformation gap, requiring proactive workforce planning to ensure equitable access to emerging opportunities in democratized roles.

docs.title: Ai job future framework
docs.text: A framework of four questions to analyze how AI is reshaping specific occupations, developed by PwC in their 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: The AI Job Future Framework, introduced in Figure 6 of PwC's 2026 report, provides a structured methodology to assess AI's occupational impact through four critical questions: (1) Expertise: How is AI changing required human expertise? (e.g., democratization reduces expertise needs while professionalization increases them); (2) Supply and Demand: How might job demand and worker supply shift as AI reshapes roles?; (3) AI's limitations: Where is human oversight needed for quality control or atypical cases?; and (4) Environmental forces: How do regulations, business bottlenecks, or ethics constrain AI adoption? The report demonstrates the framework's application across diverse occupations: For [[pharmacists]], AI handles prescription processing (Expertise shift) but humans manage patient counseling (AI's limitations); for [[software-developers]], AI generates code but humans focus on architecture (Environmental forces). Figure 9 validates the framework by showing how high AI exposure roles undergo 2.2x faster skill changes, directly addressing the Supply and Demand dimension. Organizations using this tool can anticipate skill shifts, redesign roles, and develop targeted training—particularly valuable for navigating the 'superstar effect' (Figure 12). The framework also explains wage dynamics: roles requiring human oversight of AI (Question 3) command higher premiums, as seen in Figure 15's 62% average AI skill premium. By systematically addressing these questions, employers can transform AI disruption into opportunity, while workers identify future-proof career paths in the evolving labor market. This methodology has become essential for strategic workforce planning in the AI era.

docs.title: Ai professionalization
docs.text: AI professionalization refers to the process where AI automates less expert tasks, leaving more expert tasks for humans.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: AI professionalization describes the counter-trend to democratization, where artificial intelligence automates routine tasks, thereby elevating expertise requirements for human workers. As documented in Figure 2 of the PwC 2026 report, this process affects 22% of global job postings. Examples include [[environmental-engineers]] (AI handles data collection but humans focus on complex modeling) and [[pharmacists]] (AI manages prescriptions but humans provide patient counseling). Figure 3 shows professionalization is especially pronounced in high AI exposure jobs, where expertise requirements increase substantially. The economic implications are significant: Figure 4 reveals professionalised roles have added skills at twice the rate of democratised roles (68% vs 33% growth 2018-2025), while Figure 5 shows 42% faster salary growth (37% vs 26% since 2021) and twice the job posting growth (39% vs 17%). Figure 11 confirms these roles maintain high human reliance with an epoch-score of 0.45. The report positions professionalization as creating high-value career pathways that reward advanced expertise, though it raises barriers to entry for new workers—a phenomenon documented in entry-level-jobs as 'seniorisation'. This trend validates the 'Expertise' dimension of the ai-job-future-framework, where AI handles standardized processes while humans focus on judgment-intensive work. Organizations achieving 'superstar' status (Figure 12) often excel at professionalization through strategic AI integration and complementary human skill development, creating sustainable competitive advantages in the AI-transformed economy.

docs.title: Seniorised roles
docs.text: The phenomenon where entry-level roles increasingly demand skills traditionally required in senior positions due to AI adoption.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: Seniorised roles describe the accelerating trend where entry-level positions now require skills traditionally associated with senior roles, driven by AI automation of foundational tasks. Figure 8 in the PwC 2026 report provides compelling evidence: in the US top AI exposure quartile, seniorised entry-level job postings are projected to grow 35% between 2019-2025, while non-seniorised roles decline by 10%. This occurs because AI tools handle routine onboarding tasks (e.g., data entry for [[inventory-clerks]] or basic analysis for [[recruiters]]), compressing career ladders and elevating entry requirements. Common senior skills now appearing in entry-level descriptions include strategic decision-making frameworks, cross-departmental collaboration, client advisory capabilities, and complex problem-solving—particularly in high-growth professionalised-jobs. Figure 7 contextualizes this within broader market trends, showing high AI exposure quartile entry-level postings have flatlined since 2022, suggesting seniorisation may be a prerequisite for growth in AI-impacted roles. The report warns this trend could exacerbate early-career inequality if not balanced with structured mentorship, as new graduates face higher barriers without prior exposure to advanced skills. However, organizations using the ai-job-future-framework can mitigate risks by designing onboarding that combines AI tools with human skill development. The phenomenon represents a key manifestation of AI's impact on career progression, with implications for education systems needing to prepare students for accelerated responsibility in the AI era.

docs.title: Superstar effect
docs.text: The top 20% of most AI-exposed companies, referred to as 'superstars', achieve a significantly higher productivity growth rate.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: The 'superstar effect' identifies a critical divergence among AI adopters: the top 20% of most AI-exposed firms achieve dramatically higher productivity gains than peers. As revealed in Figure 12 of the PwC 2026 report, these 'superstar' companies attained 163% average productivity growth in 2025—5 times higher than the 33.5% growth rate of most AI-exposed companies as a whole. This advantage has widened steadily since 2022, with superstars accelerating their lead while other AI-exposed firms maintain moderate growth. Figure 13 shows this productivity advantage translates to headcount growth, with superstar firms expanding employment 52.2% by 2025 versus 35.7% for least exposed quartiles. Crucially, Figure 14 demonstrates these gains are shared with workers through higher wage growth (24.4% vs 16.6% in 2025). The report attributes superstar status to three factors: strategic AI integration beyond automation (leveraging the ai-job-future-framework), investment in complementary human skills, and organizational agility in addressing [[ai's limitations]]. This creates a 'winner-takes-most' dynamic where superstars capture disproportionate value—evident in Figure 16's AI specialist hiring surge within these firms. The effect has profound implications: companies failing to achieve superstar status risk falling behind in the AI transformation, while workers in superstar firms gain through both wages and career opportunities in high-growth professionalised-jobs. Policymakers must address resulting market concentration through targeted support for mid-tier AI adopters.

docs.title: Entities
docs.text:
Companies, research firms, tools, and platforms mentioned:

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Lightcast
docs.text: Lightcast (formerly Emsi Burning Glass) is a labor market analytics company that provides detailed data on job postings, skills demand, and workforce trends. The company aggregates and analyzes millions of job postings from across the internet to create comprehensive labor market intelligence.

docs.title: Role in AI Research
docs.text: Lightcast data serves as a primary source for PwC's Global AI Jobs Barometer analysis. According to the 2026 report, the analysis examined over one billion online job advertisements across six continents, with Lightcast providing the underlying job posting data.

docs.title: Data Collection Methodology
docs.text:
Lightcast gathers job posting data through:

  • Web scraping of major job boards and company career sites
  • Partnerships with job posting aggregators
  • Direct feeds from some large employers
  • Historical archives dating back to 2012 for certain markets

The company processes this raw data to extract structured information about required skills, qualifications, compensation, and other job characteristics.

docs.title: Application in PwC's Research
docs.text:
In the PwC 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, Lightcast data was used to:

  • Track changes in job posting volumes across different AI exposure levels
  • Analyze skill requirements and how they evolve over time
  • Calculate wage premiums for AI-related skills
  • Identify trends in entry-level hiring patterns
  • Measure growth in AI specialist positions

docs.title: Key Insights Enabled by Lightcast Data
docs.text:
Analysis of Lightcast data revealed several critical patterns:

  • AI specialist job postings grew eight times faster than total job postings from 2012-2025
  • Entry-level job postings in the highest AI exposure quartile flatlined after 2022
  • Skills needed for the most AI-exposed jobs are changing 2.2x faster than for the least exposed jobs
  • Wage premiums for AI skills reached 62% across all sectors in 2025

docs.title: Limitations and Considerations
docs.text:
While valuable, Lightcast data has certain limitations:

  • Primarily captures formal, online job postings (may miss informal hiring)
  • Coverage varies by country and time period
  • Skill extraction relies on natural language processing of job descriptions
  • May not fully capture internal mobility or promotions

Researchers using Lightcast data, like PwC, typically note these limitations and adjust their methodologies accordingly.

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, pwc-ai-occupational-exposure-index

docs.title: ORBIS
docs.text: ORBIS is a comprehensive global business information database maintained by Bureau van Dijk (a Moody's Analytics company). It provides detailed financial, ownership, and operational data on millions of companies worldwide, serving as a critical resource for business intelligence and economic research.

docs.title: Role in AI Research
docs.text: In PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, ORBIS data served as the primary source for company-level analysis, particularly regarding productivity, headcount, and wage growth metrics across different levels of AI exposure.

docs.title: Data Coverage
docs.text:
ORBIS includes information on:

  • Over 400 million companies globally
  • Financial statements and performance metrics
  • Ownership structures and corporate hierarchies
  • Industry classifications using standard systems like NAICS
  • Employment data and executive information

For the PwC analysis, the database provided longitudinal data from 2018-2025 for companies with consistent reporting.

docs.title: Application in PwC's Research
docs.text:
PwC used ORBIS data to:

  • Measure productivity growth (revenue per employee) across AI exposure levels
  • Track headcount changes at companies in different AI exposure quartiles
  • Analyze wage growth patterns relative to AI adoption
  • Identify the "superstar effect" among highly AI-exposed companies

docs.title: Key Insights Enabled by ORBIS Data
docs.text:
Analysis of ORBIS data revealed several critical patterns:

  • The most AI-exposed companies showed 33.5% productivity growth (2018-2025) compared to 18.7% for least exposed
  • Headcount growth at most AI-exposed companies reached 52.2% versus 23.3% for least exposed
  • Wage growth at most AI-exposed companies reached 24.4% versus 5.6% for least exposed
  • The top 20% of most AI-exposed companies ("superstars") achieved 163% productivity growth

docs.title: Methodological Considerations
docs.text:
PwC's analysis using ORBIS data included several important methodological notes:

  • Focus on larger formal firms with annual turnover of at least $50m USD
  • Exclusion of companies that exited the market during the period (creating survivorship bias)
  • Potential bias toward larger, more formalized companies in some countries
  • Company AI exposure determined by NAICS sector classification

These considerations mean the analysis reflects patterns among established businesses rather than the entire economy, but still provides valuable insights into how AI adoption correlates with business performance.

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, superstar-effect

docs.title: PwC
docs.text: PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) is a multinational professional services network and one of the Big Four accounting firms. It provides audit and assurance, tax, and advisory services to corporations, governments, and non-profit organizations globally. With operations in 157 countries and over 360,000 employees, PwC serves as a leading authority on economic and labor market trends.

docs.title: Relevance to AI and Market Research
docs.text: PwC has emerged as a pivotal publisher of research on artificial intelligence's impact on the global labor market. Its annual Global AI Jobs Barometer report analyzes trends using data from over one billion job advertisements, providing critical insights for the market research industry. The firm's proprietary pwc-ai-occupational-exposure-index methodology has become a standard for measuring AI's workforce impact.

docs.title: Key Contributions
docs.text:
• Developed the epoch-score framework for measuring human capability reliance in occupations
  • Identified the dual trends of ai-democratization (52% of jobs) and ai-professionalization (22% of jobs)
  • Documented the superstar-effect where top AI-adopting companies achieve disproportionate productivity gains
  • Quantified wage premiums for ai-skills across economic sectors

PwC's research directly informs talent strategy for market research firms navigating AI adoption. Their data provides empirical validation for skills gap analyses and workforce planning.

Update 2026-06-25: Added reference to 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer findings. Source: PwC (2026). Global AI Jobs Barometer.

docs.title: Methodologies
docs.text:
Research methodologies used in studies:

Page First Observed Last Updated Description epoch-score 2026-06-24 2026-06-25 The EPOCH score measures the reliance on human capabilities in various occupations. pwc-ai-occupational-exposure-index 2026-06-25 2026-06-25 PwC's proprietary methodology to measure the degree to which different occupations are exposed to AI capabilities.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Epoch score
docs.text: The EPOCH score measures the reliance on human capabilities in various occupations.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: The EPOCH score, PwC's proprietary metric, quantifies occupational reliance on human capabilities difficult for AI to replicate. Ranging 0-1, it evaluates dimensions including empathy, creativity, physical dexterity, and face-to-face interaction through job description analysis and expert assessment. Figure 10 of the 2026 report provides definitive validation: new tasks added to AI-exposed roles since 2022 show EPOCH scores 2.5 times higher in the top exposure quartile (0.47) versus bottom quartile (0.19), proving AI shifts work toward human-intensive activities. Figure 11 further demonstrates its significance by showing democratised roles (0.48) and professionalised roles (0.45) maintain substantially higher scores than low exposure roles (0.26), indicating both AI impact pathways increase human reliance. The methodology explains key economic trends: occupations with higher EPOCH scores in AI-exposed roles command greater wage premiums (Figure 15), as human skills become scarcer value drivers. Crucially, the score helps operationalize the ai-job-future-framework's 'AI's limitations' dimension by identifying tasks requiring human oversight. The report shows EPOCH scores are rising fastest in high-exposure roles (Figure 9), with the top quartile's score increasing 116% from 2023-2025. This metric has become essential for organizations mapping future skill requirements, particularly in designing professionalised-jobs and addressing the human elements of 'superstar' company success (Figure 12). Policymakers also use it to target workforce development initiatives where human capabilities provide sustainable competitive advantages.

docs.title: Pwc ai occupational exposure index
docs.text: PwC's proprietary methodology to measure the degree to which different occupations are exposed to AI capabilities.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: PwC's AI Occupational Exposure Index quantifies occupational susceptibility to AI through a multi-dimensional methodology. Ranging 0-1, it analyzes 2,000+ work activities across 800+ occupations, assessing technical feasibility, economic viability, and adoption timelines for AI automation. The 2026 report reveals 74% of jobs have meaningful exposure (quartiles 2-4), with the top quartile experiencing transformative impacts. Figure 3 demonstrates how the index interacts with expertise requirements, showing high-exposure jobs either increase (professionalisation) or decrease (democratization) expertise demands. The methodology underpins critical findings: Figure 7 uses it to segment entry-level job trends, Figure 9 to analyze skill change velocity (top quartile changing 2.2x faster), and Figure 12 to correlate exposure with productivity. Notably, the index evolved to incorporate generative AI capabilities post-2022, explaining accelerated impacts observed after ChatGPT's release. Organizations apply it to identify at-risk roles, prioritize reskilling, and benchmark against industry peers—particularly valuable for navigating the 'superstar effect' where top-quartile firms achieve 163% productivity growth. The report details sector-specific adaptations (e.g., healthcare's focus on diagnostic support roles), making it versatile for workforce planning. When combined with the epoch-score, the index forms the empirical foundation of the ai-job-future-framework, enabling organizations to systematically address all four framework questions. Its longitudinal design (2012-2025 data) provides unprecedented insight into AI's accelerating labor market transformation.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Market research job roles, positions, and career paths:

Page First Observed Last Updated Description entry-level-jobs 2026-06-24 2026-06-25 Entry-level job postings in quartiles with lower AI exposure continue to grow, while those in the highest AI exposure quartile have flatlined since 2022. professionalised-jobs 2026-06-25 2026-06-25 Professionalised jobs are occupations reshaped by AI to demand more human expertise, representing 22% of advertised positions globally. democratised-jobs 2026-06-25 2026-06-25 Democratised jobs are occupations reshaped by AI to demand less human expertise, representing 52% of advertised positions globally.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Ai specialist roles
docs.text: Job roles specifically focused on the development, implementation, and maintenance of AI systems, growing eight times faster than total job postings.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: AI specialist roles are experiencing explosive growth, expanding eight times faster than total job postings according to Figure 16 of the PwC 2026 report. From a 2012 baseline of zero, these roles reached 11.0x growth by 2025 versus 2.0x for total postings, with acceleration intensifying post-2022. Figure 16b shows pervasive sector adoption: Technology, Media and Telecoms leads at 11.4% of sector roles, followed by Health Industries (4.0%), Financial Services (3.0%), and Manufacturing (2.0%). Core responsibilities include AI system architecture, prompt engineering, ethical governance, and domain-specific integration—often blending technical and business skills. Figure 15 confirms these roles command significant wage premiums averaging 62%, with Consumer Markets reaching 118%. The report identifies three growth drivers: urgent demand for AI implementation expertise, the need to address [[ai's limitations]] in quality control and edge cases, and regulatory compliance requirements. Crucially, Figure 12 links this growth to the 'superstar effect', as top-performing AI-exposed companies achieve 163% productivity growth by strategically deploying these specialists. Unlike traditional tech roles, modern AI specialists increasingly operate in hybrid functions (e.g., AI-augmented [[pharmacists]] or [[financial-analysts]]), requiring both domain knowledge and technical proficiency. This trend positions AI specialization as a critical career pathway across the economy, with implications for education systems needing to develop curricula aligned with the ai-job-future-framework.

docs.title: Democratised jobs
docs.text: Democratised jobs are occupations reshaped by AI to demand less human expertise, representing 52% of advertised positions globally.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: Democratised jobs—where AI automates more expert tasks, leaving less expert tasks for humans—constitute 52% of global postings but show distinct market patterns. Figure 2 illustrates this through roles like [[inventory-clerks]] (AI automates inventory management but humans move stock) and [[recruiters]] (AI screens CVs but humans negotiate contracts). While numerous, Figure 4 shows they've added skills at half the pace of professionalised roles (33% vs 68% growth 2018-2025), and Figure 5 confirms slower salary growth (26% vs 37% since 2021). However, Figure 11 reveals a critical nuance: democratised roles maintain the highest average epoch-score (0.48), indicating continued reliance on human capabilities for remaining tasks—often involving physical dexterity, adaptability in unstructured environments, or interpersonal interactions. This explains why these roles remain resilient despite reduced expertise requirements. The report warns that while democratisation expands job accessibility (e.g., enabling non-experts to perform complex analysis via AI tools), it may compress career ladders without structured progression paths. Figure 9 contextualizes this within skills transformation, showing democratised roles still experience 2.2x faster skill changes than low-exposure jobs. Organizations can leverage this trend through the ai-job-future-framework's 'Environmental forces' dimension, designing roles that combine AI efficiency with uniquely human execution—particularly valuable in sectors like retail and logistics where physical interaction remains essential.

docs.title: Entry level jobs
docs.text: Entry-level job postings in quartiles with lower AI exposure continue to grow, while those in the highest AI exposure quartile have flatlined since 2022.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: The PwC 2026 report reveals a stark bifurcation in entry-level job market dynamics. Figure 7 shows postings in the bottom three AI exposure quartiles grew steadily from 2012-2025 (reaching 6.0-8.0x 2012 baselines), while the highest exposure quartile flatlined at 5.0x after 2022—coinciding with ChatGPT's release. This stagnation affects roles most susceptible to AI automation, such as basic data processing and routine analysis. Figure 8 further analyzes this by distinguishing 'seniorised' versus non-seniorised roles within the top quartile: seniorised entry-level positions (requiring traditionally senior skills) are projected to grow 35% between 2019-2025 in the US, while non-seniorised roles decline by 10%. This divergence occurs because AI handles foundational tasks (e.g., automated CV screening for [[recruiters]]), raising entry barriers for new workers. The report warns this trend could exacerbate early-career inequality, particularly for candidates without access to advanced training. However, it also creates opportunities in high-growth sectors like Technology where ai-specialist-roles are expanding rapidly. Figure 9 contextualizes this within skills transformation, showing AI-exposed roles undergo 2.2x faster skill changes, forcing entry-level candidates to continuously adapt. Organizations using the ai-job-future-framework can mitigate risks by designing structured onboarding that balances AI tools with human skill development.

docs.title: Professionalised jobs
docs.text: Professionalised jobs are occupations reshaped by AI to demand more human expertise, representing 22% of advertised positions globally.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: Professionalised jobs—where AI automates less expert tasks, leaving more expert tasks for humans—show robust positive dynamics per the PwC 2026 report. Figure 4 demonstrates these roles have demanded additional skills at twice the rate of democratised roles (68% vs 33% growth 2018-2025), with the gap widening significantly since 2022. Figure 5 confirms they've achieved 42% faster salary growth (37% vs 26% since 2021) and twice the job posting growth (39% vs 17% from 2018-2025). Figure 11 reveals their high average epoch-score of 0.45, indicating substantial reliance on human capabilities despite elevated expertise requirements. The report identifies exemplar roles like [[environmental-engineers]] (where AI handles data collection but humans focus on complex modeling) and [[pharmacists]] (where AI manages prescriptions but humans provide patient counseling). These occupations thrive because AI handles routine analysis, freeing humans for strategic judgment and innovation—validating the 'Expertise' dimension of the ai-job-future-framework. Figure 13 shows professionalised-job employers also achieve 52.2% headcount growth by 2025 (versus 35.7% for least exposed firms), suggesting these roles drive organizational expansion. The data positions professionalisation as a high-value career pathway, though it requires continuous upskilling. Workers in these roles increasingly need to address [[ai's limitations]] through quality oversight and atypical case management, creating sustainable competitive advantages in the AI economy.

docs.text:
Skills, competencies, and requirements found in job listings:

Page First Observed Last Updated Description human-intensive-skills 2026-06-24 2026-06-25 Human-intensive skills such as empathy, creativity, and face-to-face presence are increasingly important in AI-exposed roles. senior-skills 2026-06-25 2026-06-25 Senior skills traditionally required in more senior positions that are now increasingly appearing in entry-level job descriptions due to AI adoption.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Ai skills
docs.text: Technical and domain-specific skills related to artificial intelligence, with wage premiums averaging 62% across sectors.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: Wage premiums for AI skills have risen to 62% (from 57% in 2025), with significant sectoral variation documented in Figure 15 of the PwC report. Consumer Markets leads at 118%, followed by Technology, Media and Telecoms (84%), Energy, Utilities and Resources (75%), Manufacturing (73%), and Professional Services (67%). Financial Services shows 53%, Health Industries 37%, and Government and Public Sector the lowest at 16%. These premiums reflect acute talent shortages in AI implementation roles, particularly for hybrid skills combining technical proficiency (prompt engineering, system integration) with domain expertise. Figure 14 confirms these premiums correlate with productivity gains at AI-exposed companies, where wage growth for AI-skilled workers accelerated to 24.4% in 2025 versus 16.6% for least exposed firms. Crucially, Figure 16 shows AI specialist job postings are growing eight times faster than total postings, with Technology, Media and Telecoms accounting for 11.4% of sector roles by 2025. The report identifies three premium drivers: scarcity of talent capable of overseeing AI quality control (addressing [[ai's limitations]] in the ai-job-future-framework), demand for skills in atypical case management, and regulatory compliance needs. This underscores AI proficiency as a critical career accelerator, especially in 'superstar' companies achieving 163% productivity growth (Figure 12).

docs.title: Human intensive skills
docs.text: Human-intensive skills such as empathy, creativity, and face-to-face presence are increasingly important in AI-exposed roles.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: The PwC 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer provides robust quantitative evidence for the accelerating criticality of human-intensive skills in AI-reshaped occupations. Figure 10 demonstrates that new tasks added to AI-exposed roles since 2022 are 2.5 times more likely to rely on capabilities like empathy, creativity, and face-to-face presence. Quantitatively, the top AI exposure quartile shows an average epoch-score of 0.47 for new tasks compared to 0.19 in the bottom quartile. This trend is reinforced in Figure 11, which reveals democratised roles (0.48) and professionalised roles (0.45) maintain significantly higher EPOCH scores than low exposure roles (0.26), indicating structural reliance on human capabilities despite AI automation. The report attributes this to AI handling standardized cognitive tasks while elevating the value of uniquely human interactions—particularly in client-facing roles like [[pharmacists]] and [[recruiters]] as shown in Figure 2. These skills now represent critical differentiators in high-exposure occupations, with implications for workforce development. Organizations increasingly seek candidates who can exercise emotional intelligence in complex scenarios where AI outputs require human validation, creating new career pathways in professionalised-jobs. The data suggests human-intensive capabilities will become non-negotiable requirements for roles experiencing significant AI transformation.

docs.title: Senior skills
docs.text: Senior skills traditionally required in more senior positions that are now increasingly appearing in entry-level job descriptions due to AI adoption.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: New data from the PwC 2026 report quantifies the acceleration of 'seniorised' entry-level roles. Figure 8 reveals that in the US top AI exposure quartile, entry-level positions requiring traditionally senior skills (strategic decision-making, complex problem-solving, leadership) are projected to grow 35% between 2019-2025, while non-seniorised roles decline by 10%. This phenomenon occurs because AI tools automate foundational tasks previously performed by junior staff—such as data entry for [[inventory-clerks]] or CV screening for [[recruiters]] (Figure 2)—effectively compressing career ladders. Consequently, new graduates must now demonstrate competencies typically associated with 3-5 years of experience. Key senior skills appearing in entry-level descriptions include advanced data interpretation, cross-functional collaboration, and client relationship management, particularly in high-growth professionalised-jobs like environmental engineering. Figure 7 contextualizes this within broader market trends, showing high AI exposure quartile entry-level postings have flatlined since 2022, suggesting seniorisation may be a prerequisite for growth in AI-impacted roles. The report warns this trend could exacerbate early-career inequality without structured mentorship programs, while creating opportunities for accelerated career progression in organizations embracing the ai-job-future-framework.

docs.title: Pwc 2026 global ai jobs barometer
docs.text: PwC's comprehensive analysis of over one billion job advertisements examining AI's impact on the global labor market.

docs.title: Update: 2026-06-27
docs.text: This update incorporates detailed analysis of all 16 figures from the report. Key empirical findings include: A two-track jobs market has emerged where 52% of jobs are being democratized (AI automates more expert tasks, leaving less expert tasks for humans) and 22% are being professionalized (AI automates less expert tasks, leaving more expert tasks for humans) (Figure 2). Entry-level job postings in the highest AI exposure quartile have flatlined since 2022 (coinciding with ChatGPT's release), while lower exposure quartiles continue robust growth (Figure 7). Skills in AI-exposed roles are changing 2.2 times faster than in least exposed roles, with the gap widening annually (Figure 9). New tasks added to AI-exposed roles since 2022 are 2.5 times more likely to rely on human-intensive capabilities like empathy and creativity, quantified through the epoch-score (Figure 10). The report identifies a pronounced 'superstar effect' where the top 20% of AI-exposed companies achieve 5 times higher productivity growth (163% average) than the most exposed companies as a whole (33.5% in 2025) (Figure 12). Wage premiums for AI skills average 62% across sectors, ranging from 118% in Consumer Markets to 16% in Government (Figure 15). AI specialist job postings are growing eight times faster than total job postings, with Technology, Media and Telecoms leading at 11.4% of sector roles (Figure 16). These findings, derived from PwC's pwc-ai-occupational-exposure-index, demonstrate AI's asymmetric impact on labor markets and validate the ai-job-future-framework's predictive power.
